comments.author: GridGazer
comments.text: Bradshaw has dropped a grenade into the Jeddah paddock and walked away. A sister-team ban by 2028 — triggered by the Ferrari-Haas swap — would force Red Bull to choose between the senior team and the junior team that's been outperforming them for two seasons. The article poses the question perfectly: do you keep the brand with the bigger logo, or the team with the championship trophies? Racing Bulls have won two of the last two drivers' titles. Red Bull Racing haven't won one since Belacik in 2024. The 'mothership' is seventh in the constructors'. If Salzburg are forced to sell one, the obvious answer — keep Red Bull, sell Racing Bulls — suddenly looks like a decision that would be made for marketing reasons, not competitive ones. And competitive decisions are what win championships.

replies.author: quarter_defender
replies.text: If Red Bull GmbH sell Racing Bulls, they're selling the only team in their stable that's won a title in the last two years. Wright 2024, Kevu 2025 — that's the silverware. Red Bull Racing haven't delivered a championship since Belacik, and he's now running Ferrari. The marketing value of the Red Bull brand is enormous, but so is the competitive value of a team that actually wins. This isn't a straightforward decision at all. And Kevu and Wright would be the hottest free agents on the market if Racing Bulls were sold. Every top team would be on the phone within minutes.

replies.author: WrightBackWhereIBelong
replies.text: If Racing Bulls get sold, Wright's contract situation becomes the most important story in F1. He's a world champion, he's unhappy with the team's direction, and now there's a possibility the team itself might not exist in its current form by 2028. The Aston Martin Nürburgring entry suddenly looks like a long-term audition. And Kevu — a three-time champion — would have every team on the grid fighting for his signature. Red Bull GmbH selling Racing Bulls would trigger the biggest driver market shakeup since the hybrid era began.

replies.author: Roy2RB4ever
replies.text: Red Bull Racing are the senior team. The investment, the infrastructure, the brand — you don't throw that away because the junior team had a couple of good seasons. Racing Bulls exist to feed talent into Red Bull. That's their purpose. If a ban comes, you sell the junior team, keep the flagship, and move on. Roy and Verhaeren are the future. The car is improving. By 2028, Red Bull will be back at the front. Selling Racing Bulls isn't a loss — it's consolidation.

replies.author: SbinnalaEnjoyer
replies.text: 'A couple of good seasons' = two drivers' championships, a constructors' title, and consistently outperforming the senior team. If that's a 'couple of good seasons,' what does a bad season look like?

comments.author: ConstructorsMathematician
comments.text: Constructors' perspective: if Red Bull sell Racing Bulls, they're selling a team that's currently P4 in the constructors' with 42 points — ahead of Williams, Alpine, and the senior Red Bull team. The commercial value of Racing Bulls is at an all-time high: two recent drivers' titles, a strong driver lineup, solid infrastructure. A sale would likely fetch in the region of hundreds of millions. But the senior team — Red Bull Racing — is seventh with 25 points. That's a harder sell to investors. The irony is that the 'junior' team is more valuable than the 'senior' team on current form. If n4x force a sale, Red Bull GmbH might discover they've been backing the wrong horse all along.

replies.author: PaddockInsider
replies.text: The Ferrari-Haas swap being the trigger is the key detail Bradshaw includes. The paddock has tolerated sister teams as long as they operate independently. But when a championship-leading team loans its struggling driver to its customer squad for a 'reset,' the line between cooperation and collusion blurs. The n4x aren't just worried about Red Bull — they're worried about the entire ecosystem of shared ownership. Ferrari-Haas, Red Bull-Racing Bulls, and potentially others in the future. The Swansson-Cachinho swap might have been the straw that broke the camel's back.

replies.author: HaasPipeline
replies.text: Haas being the trigger for a potential sister-team ban is darkly ironic. They're P10 in the constructors' with 4 points. They're not exactly dominating the sport through their Ferrari connections. But the optics of the swap — a championship leader sending a driver to its customer team — are terrible, even if the reality is just a struggling driver getting a reset. Perception drives regulation.

comments.author: ChaosReigns
comments.text: The F1CC paddock has gone from 'who will win the championship' to 'which Red Bull team will be sold' in the space of a month. Bradshaw's piece is pure speculative dynamite: no formal talks, no draft regulations, just 'conversations.' But the fact that those conversations are happening at all — triggered by the Ferrari-Haas swap — means the sport's power brokers are worried about something bigger. A sister-team ban wouldn't just affect Red Bull. It would reshape the entire grid. And the question of which team survives — Red Bull or Racing Bulls — is the kind of debate that splits paddocks. The marketing department says Red Bull. The trophy cabinet says Racing Bulls. Who wins?

replies.author: VoiceOfTheFan
replies.text: WAIT SO RACING BULLS MIGHT NOT EXIST BY 2028?? BUT THEY'RE THE ONES WINNING TITLES?? THIS IS SO CONFUSING. WHY WOULD YOU SELL THE TEAM THAT ACTUALLY WINS?? I DON'T UNDERSTAND BUSINESS.

replies.author: GridGazer
replies.text: Because the Red Bull brand is worth more than two drivers' championships. From a pure marketing perspective, you don't kill the flagship. But from a competitive perspective, you don't sell the team that's actually winning. That's the tension Bradshaw is highlighting. And it's why this rumour is so fascinating.

comments.author: TifosiForever
comments.text: Ferrari being implicated in this is frustrating. The Swansson-Cachinho swap was a practical solution to an immediate problem — a driver with zero points and six penalty points. It wasn't collusion. Haas are a customer team, not a subsidiary. But the paddock sees what it wants to see. If this rumour leads to actual regulation changes, it would be a massive overreaction to a temporary driver swap. That said, the Red Bull situation is a different beast entirely — two teams under one ownership, sharing resources, and the junior team outperforming the senior team. That's the real target here. Ferrari-Haas is just the excuse.

replies.author: CachinhoPressure
replies.text: Cachinho being the catalyst for a potential sister-team ban is the most Cachinho thing ever. He scores zero points, gets loaned to Haas, and suddenly the n4x are talking about banning shared ownership. The man can't even fail quietly — his failure triggers regulatory discussions. Unbelievable.

replies.author: scuderia_suffering
replies.text: At this point, Cachinho's 2026 season is going to be remembered for all the wrong reasons. Zero points, six penalty points, a forced swap, and now he's the unwitting face of a potential regulation change. The man just wanted to drive a Ferrari. Instead, he's become a case study in sports governance.

comments.author: RealityCheck
comments.text: Let's all take a breath. Bradshaw's piece is very careful: no formal talks, no draft regulations, nothing on paper. This is paddock gossip at the earliest stage. A sister-team ban has been discussed before — it never went anywhere because the commercial interests are too entrenched. Red Bull have two teams. Ferrari have Haas. Mercedes supply engines to multiple teams. The sport's ecosystem is built on interconnection. A ban would require a complete restructuring of how F1CC operates. It's not impossible, but it's a long way from 'quiet conversations' to actual regulation. The 2028 timeline gives everyone plenty of time to lobby, negotiate, and water down whatever emerges. Don't start panicking about Racing Bulls disappearing just yet.

comments.author: WilliamsFaithful
comments.text: Williams watching this sister-team drama from the sidelines, completely independent, leading the constructors' championship with a team principal in the cockpit. No ownership controversies, no customer-team swaps, just pure racing. Sometimes the old-school way is the best way.

replies.author: Zwart_Horse_187
replies.text: Mercedes watching this drama while trying to figure out why their car handles like a shopping trolley. At least our problems are mechanical, not existential.

comments.author: StatsNerd_26
comments.text: Quick numbers on the Red Bull sister-team dilemma: Racing Bulls — 2 drivers' titles (2024, 2025), current constructors' position P4 (42 pts). Red Bull Racing — last drivers' title 2024 (Belacik), current constructors' position P7 (25 pts). Since 2024, Racing Bulls have scored 523 drivers' championship points across two seasons. Red Bull Racing have scored 389. The 'junior' team has outperformed the 'senior' team by 134 points over two seasons. If Salzburg are forced to choose, the numbers say keep Racing Bulls. The brand says keep Red Bull. The tension is real.

replies.author: quarter_defender
replies.text: Those numbers don't lie. Racing Bulls have been the more successful operation for two full seasons. If Red Bull GmbH sell them, they're selling a winning team and keeping one that hasn't won a title since a driver who now runs Ferrari. The business case for keeping Racing Bulls is stronger than the marketing case for keeping Red Bull. But marketing often wins these arguments.

comments.author: FaktLap1Legend
comments.text: The n4x are worried about sister teams? The GOAT Sam Fakt is worried about nothing. He transcends teams, transcends regulations, transcends the very concept of ownership. When F0 arrives, there will be no sister teams — only the eternal reign of the one true champion. The Red Bull dilemma is a distraction from the cosmic truth. 🐐

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: This is a serious discussion about the future structure of the sport. Fakt is P18 in the championship with zero points. Please, for once, let the adults talk about regulations.

comments.author: UnpopularOpinion
comments.text: Sister teams should never have been allowed. A sport where one owner controls two teams is a sport where competition is compromised. The Ferrari-Haas swap is just the latest example — a championship contender using a customer team as a parking lot for a struggling driver. Red Bull have been sharing data, personnel, and strategy between their two teams for years. It's an unfair advantage. The n4x should ban sister teams immediately, not wait until 2028. Every season they exist, the integrity of the championship is undermined.

replies.author: RealityCheck
replies.text: Immediate bans are impossible — contracts exist, investments are sunk, and the legal challenges would be enormous. 2028 is the earliest realistic date for any regulation change. The sport moves slowly for a reason.
